evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore (lines 468-473) and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti (lines 606-610) wrap parseInt/parseDouble in try/catch for NumberFormatException, but sibling handlers handleSemanticField (line 708-709), handleSemanticFieldExamples (line 786), and handleConcordanceExamples (line 813) use bare parseInt/parseDouble without guards — inconsistent error handling in sibling HTTP handlers

BlackLabQueryExecutor.getTotalFrequency: catches broad Exception, logs a warning, and silently returns 0; callers compute logDice with 0 total frequency, producing a score of 0.0 rather than surfacing that frequency lookup failed — silent swallowing that corrupts downstream scoring

SemanticFieldExplorer.close(): catch (Exception e) { throw new IOException(e); } — appropriate wrapping but uses maximally broad Exception catch when the underlying close() is known to throw checked exceptions
BlackLabConllUIndexer.errorOccurred(): logs and returns true to continue — intentional silencing of per-file index errors, but callers have no way to discover how many files failed
WordSketchApiServer.start(): wraps IOException in RuntimeException — intentional for startup failure, but this diverges from the IOException-first strategy used everywhere else

InvalidQuery in BlackLabQueryExecutor is consistently wrapped into IOException with message context preserved (e.g., 'BCQL parse error: ' + e.getMessage(), e) — good cause-chain preservation where it is applied

HTTP handlers in WordSketchApiServer use two tiers: some catch both IOException and Exception separately (explore, explore-multi), others catch only IOException (concordance examples, semantic field), and the catch-all Exception tier is absent from some handlers — error surface is uneven

strengths: IOException is used as the primary checked exception throughout the query layer; InvalidQuery and other BlackLab exceptions are consistently wrapped with cause preserved
HTTP handlers log errors with the full exception before converting to JSON 500 responses — error information is not silently lost at the API boundary
GrammarConfigLoader uses IOException with descriptive messages for all configuration validation failures, giving callers actionable context
error_consistency.issue_character: The dominant pattern is sibling inconsistency: handlers that look nearly identical differ in whether they guard numeric parameter parsing, and the core frequency-lookup path silently swallows errors that would corrupt logDice results rather than propagating them.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase has a clear primary strategy (IOException wrapping) and applies it well in the query layer, but HTTP handler siblings diverge noticeably — some guard NumberFormatException, others don't, and the most consequential silent swallow is in getTotalFrequency which silently returns 0 when frequency lookup fails, producing incorrect logDice scores without any caller-visible signal. A codebase scoring above 70 would have consistent guard patterns across sibling handlers; above 80 would have no silent-swallow on correctness-critical paths. This falls below those thresholds.

issues.summary: handleSemanticField, handleSemanticFieldExamples, and handleConcordanceExamples parse numeric URL params without NumberFormatException guard, unlike their sibling handlers
evidence: Contrast: lines 468-473 in handleSemanticFieldExplore and lines 606-610 in handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti both wrap all parseInt/parseDouble in try/catch(NumberFormatException)
issues.suggestion: Wrap all parseInt/parseDouble calls in handleSemanticField, handleSemanticFieldExamples, and handleConcordanceExamples with try/catch(NumberFormatException) that returns 400 with an error message, consistent with sibling handlers.

issues.summary: getTotalFrequency silently swallows lookup exceptions and returns 0, causing callers to compute incorrect logDice scores without any failure signal
evidence: getTotalFrequency: catch (Exception e) { logger.warn("Failed to retrieve frequency..."); return 0; }
LogDiceCalculator.compute: when headwordFreq or collocateTotal is 0, returns 0.0 — callers receive a logDice of 0.0 and cannot tell whether the word is rare or the lookup failed
buildAndRankCollocates calls getTotalFrequency per collocate in a loop — a systematic failure would silently zero out all scores
issues.suggestion: Either propagate the IOException from getTotalFrequency (declaring it throws IOException), or have the catch return a negative sentinel and document that callers must treat negatives as 'unknown'; do not silently return a valid-looking 0.

issues.summary: BlackLabConllUIndexer.errorOccurred returns true to continue indexing but callers have no way to discover how many files failed
evidence: errorOccurred: logger.error("Indexing error in {}: {}", path, e.getMessage()); return true; // continue indexing
documentCount and tokenCount are incremented in fileDone, but there is no errorCount AtomicLong — callers only see total success counts, not failure counts
getDocumentCount() and getTokenCount() are public; there is no getErrorCount() method
issues.suggestion: Add an AtomicLong errorCount field, increment it in errorOccurred, and expose getErrorCount() so callers can assert or log how many files were skipped on errors.

root_causes: HTTP handler methods were written incrementally; later handlers added NFE guards but earlier sibling handlers were not retroactively updated
getTotalFrequency chose to return a safe default (0) to keep the hot-path working, but the silent-0 contract is not documented and corrupts scoring correctness
